doc.title: Allocate dividends — preferred vs common
doc.event: The board declares $20,000 of dividends. Preferred stock: 1,000 shares, $100 par, 6% — a $6,000-per-year claim — and NOTHING was paid for the last two years. How much reaches common depends entirely on one word in the preferred contract: cumulative.

variants.label: Cumulative — two years of arrears must be paid first

entries.caption: Declare $20,000: arrears $12,000 + current $6,000 to preferred, remainder to common

lines.label: total declared
lines.why: One declaration, one RE debit — the allocation battle happens on the credit side.
lines.trap: Arrears themselves were never booked as a liability while unpaid; they only become one NOW, inside this declaration.

lines.label: 12,000 arrears + 6,000 current
lines.why: Cumulative preferred eats in strict order: all skipped years (2 × 6,000), then the current 6,000 — before common sees a cent.
lines.trap: Count the arrears years carefully; 'two years in arrears' means two FULL missed preferences on top of this year's.

lines.label: whatever survives
lines.trap: If the declaration were 18,000 or less, common's line simply wouldn't exist — preferred can absorb an entire declaration.

computationPaths.narration: Cumulative waterfall: arrears (2 × 1,000 × $100 × 6% = 12,000) → current preference (6,000) → common gets the remainder (2,000).

variants.label: Noncumulative — the skipped years are simply gone

entries.caption: Declare $20,000: only the CURRENT $6,000 preference exists

lines.label: total declared
lines.trap: The word 'noncumulative' erases history — it does not erase the current-year preference.

lines.label: current year only
lines.why: Noncumulative preferred's skipped dividends vanish forever — only this year's 6,000 preference stands.
lines.trap: Paying the 12,000 'arrears' here hands preferred money it has no legal claim to — the flip side of the cumulative error.

lines.label: the rest
lines.trap: Same facts, one contract word different, and common's take goes 2,000 → 14,000. That swing IS the exam question.

memorize.body: Annual preference = shares × par × rate. Cumulative: (arrears years × preference) + current preference first, common gets the residual. Noncumulative: current preference only.
Dividends in arrears are NOT a liability and never appear in the accounts — they're a footnote disclosure until a declaration finally includes them.
If the preferred is also PARTICIPATING, it re-enters the pool after common gets a matching rate — rare on exams, but read the contract line before allocating.

questions.prompt: $20,000 is declared. Preferred: 1,000 shares, $100 par, 6%, CUMULATIVE, two years in arrears. Common receives…

distractors.feedback: 14,000 ignores the two years of arrears — that's the NONCUMULATIVE answer. Cumulative preferred collects 12,000 of skipped dividends plus the current 6,000 before common's turn: 20,000 − 18,000 = 2,000.
That treats arrears as ONE missed year. Two years in arrears = 2 × 6,000 = 12,000, plus the current 6,000 → preferred takes 18,000, leaving common 2,000.

build.scaffold: Allocation before entry: compute the annual preference (shares × par × rate), decide what history counts (cumulative or not), pay preferred its full claim, and let common have the remainder. Then the entry is one debit, two credits.
